one_liner: A 38-year-old male with a history of recurrent kidney stones presents with acute-onset severe left flank pain radiating to the groin.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient reports sudden onset of severe, colicky left flank pain that started 3 hours ago and radiates to the left lower quadrant and groin. He rates it 9/10 and describes it as the worst pain of his life. He is unable to find a comfortable position. He has had 2 episodes of emesis. He noticed pink-tinged urine before coming to the ED. He reports a prior calcium oxalate stone 3 years ago that passed spontaneously. He admits to poor fluid intake and a diet high in red meat and spinach. No fever, dysuria, or urinary retention.
stage2.pmh: Recurrent nephrolithiasis (1 prior stone), obesity, gout.
stage2.medications: Ibuprofen PRN for joint pain, multivitamin with calcium.
stage2.family_history: Father had kidney stones. Mother has type 2 diabetes.
stage2.social_history: Software engineer. Drinks 2-3 cups of coffee daily, limited water intake. Occasional beer. No tobacco or illicit drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: Male in obvious distress, constantly shifting position on the bed. No diaphoresis. Heart: tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs: clear bilaterally. Abdomen: soft, non-distended, mild left lower quadrant tenderness, no rebound or guarding, no palpable masses; **Severe left costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion**. Extremities: no edema.

lab_results.result: pH 5.5, >50 RBCs/hpf, no WBCs, no bacteria, calcium oxalate crystals (envelope-shaped)
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hematuria with calcium oxalate crystals strongly suggests calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis
Normal renal function; no evidence of obstructive nephropathy
Upper normal; should prompt evaluation for hypercalciuria
Hyperuricemia may contribute to stone formation through heterogeneous nucleation

imaging_results.result: 5 mm radiopaque calculus in the left proximal ureter at the ureteropelvic junction with mild left-sided hydronephrosis. No additional stones. No other abnormalities.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Gold standard imaging confirms obstructing ureteral stone; 5 mm stones have ~70% chance of spontaneous passage

imaging_results.result: Radiopaque density projecting over the left renal area consistent with calcification.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Calcium oxalate stones are radiopaque on plain film; useful for follow-up monitoring

acceptable_tests: 24-hour urine stone risk panel (for recurrent stone formers)

stage5.final_clue: A 5 mm obstructing left ureteral stone on non-contrast CT with calcium oxalate crystals on urinalysis, in a patient with recurrent stones, obesity, high-oxalate diet, and low fluid intake,.

debrief.classic_presentation: A middle-aged male with sudden-onset severe colicky flank pain radiating to the groin, with nausea/vomiting, hematuria, and inability to find a comfortable position. Risk factors include male sex, obesity, low fluid intake, high-protein and high-oxalate diet, and family history of stones.
debrief.pathophysiology: Calcium oxalate stones form when urine becomes supersaturated with calcium and oxalate, creating crystals that aggregate into stones in the renal collecting system. Risk factors include low urine volume (dehydration), hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria (dietary or enteric), hyperuricosuria (uric acid promotes calcium oxalate nucleation), and hypocitraturia (citrate inhibits crystallization). When a stone migrates from the kidney into the ureter, it causes obstruction and ureteral spasm, producing severe colicky pain that follows the dermatomal distribution of the ureter.
debrief.treatment_overview: Acute management: aggressive pain control with NSAIDs (ketorolac first-line) and/or opioids, IV fluids, antiemetics. Medical expulsive therapy with tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) for distal ureteral stones <10 mm. Stones <5 mm usually pass spontaneously. Stones >10 mm or those causing persistent obstruction, infection, or intractable symptoms require urological intervention (shock wave lithotripsy or ureteroscopy). Prevention: increase fluid intake to >2.5 L/day, dietary modifications (reduce sodium and animal protein, increase citrus fruits), and potassium citrate for hypocitraturia.
high_yield_pearls: Calcium oxalate stones are the most common type of kidney stone (~80%) and appear as envelope-shaped crystals on urinalysis.
Non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis is the gold standard imaging study for nephrolithiasis (sensitivity >95%).
Stones <5 mm have a ~70-90% spontaneous passage rate; stones 5-10 mm have ~50% rate; stones >10 mm rarely pass and usually need intervention.
The three natural narrowing points where stones commonly lodge: ureteropelvic junction, pelvic brim (where ureter crosses iliac vessels), and ureterovesical junction.
Hypercalciuria is the most common metabolic abnormality in calcium stone formers. Thiazide diuretics reduce urinary calcium and are used for prevention.
sources: AUA/Endourological Society Guideline: Surgical Management of Stones (2022)
UpToDate: Kidney stones in adults — diagnosis and acute management (2024)
Curhan GC. Epidemiology of stone disease. Urol Clin North Am. 2007;34(3):287-293.

after_stage2_more_likely: Nephrolithiasis — classic presentation with severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin, hematuria, CVA tenderness, history of prior stones
Ureteral colic — symptoms are consistent regardless of stone composition
after_stage2_less_likely: Testicular torsion — intact cremasteric reflex, no scrotal tenderness or swelling
Appendicitis — pain is left-sided and radiates to groin; no peritoneal signs
after_labs_top3: Calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis — calcium oxalate crystals on UA, obstructing stone on CT, radiopaque stone
Uric acid stone — hyperuricemia present but stone is radiopaque (uric acid stones are radiolucent)
Pyelonephritis — no fever, no WBCs in urine, no leukocytosis
